content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 4,
1992
Cliff Leo Tiliman, Jr.
Field Representative
United Service Employees Local
616
337
17th Street,
#204
Oakland,
CA
94612
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1—92-226
Dear Mr.
Tiliman:
You have requested advice under the conflict of interest code
provisions of the Political
Reform Act.’!
Since your letter is
a
request for general
information only,
we are treating it as
a
request for informal assistance.
2
!
2!JESTIONS
1.
Who governs what classifications are to be included in
the conflict of interest code?
2.
Does the Fair Political Practices Commission maintain
copies of conflict of interest codes developed
by county
governments?
3.
Does the Conflict of Interest Code for the Alameda County
Assessor’s Office exceed the requirements of the Political Reform
Act?
4.
Is
a Report of Outside Employment required
by the
provisions of the Political Reform Act,
and if so,
how many other
counties use such a report?
1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
• P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660


Page Two
Advice File No.
1—92-226
CONCLUSIONS
1.
The Alameda County Board of Supervisors is the code
reviewing body for all agencies within Alameda County and has the
authority to approve, modify or reject the provisions of a
conflict of interest code within its jurisdiction.
2.
The Fair Political Practices Commission does not maintain
copies of conflict of interest codes for county governments.
3.
It appears that the Conflict of Interest Code for the
Alameda County Assessor’s Office does not exceed the requirements
of the Political Reform Act.
3
?
4.
The Political Reform Act does not require the filing of a
Report of Outside Employment.
ANALYSIS
The United Services Employees International Union Local
616
represents professional and clerical employees within the Alameda
County Assessor’s Office.
You have questions regarding the
recently adopted conflict of interest code for the office of the
County Assessor.
We will answer each question as it appears in
your letter.
1.
Who governs what classifications are to be included in
the conflict of interest code?
No provision of
a conflict of interest code shall be
effective until it has been approved by its respective code
reviewing body.
A code reviewing body may approve
a proposed
code;
review a proposed code and approve it as revised;
or return
a proposed code to an agency for revision and resubmission.
(Section 87309.)
The code reviewing body for any county agency,
and of any local government agency with jurisdiction wholly within
the county,
is the county board of supervisors.
(Section
82011(b).)
It should be noted,
though, that the Act does provide for
additional review of the actions of an agency or of a code
reviewing body.
Any person may petition an agency for
reconsideration of its code.
If the petition is denied the
petitioner may appeal to the code reviewing body.
The code
reviewing body then has
90 days to either dismiss the appeal or
issue any appropriate order to the agency.
(Section 87307.)
The Conflict of Interest Code for the Alameda County
Assessor’s Office commences at Section 4.01(C)
of the attached
Office of Assessor Personnel Manual.


Page Three
Advice File No.
1—92—226
Judicial review of any action of a code reviewing body may be
further pursued by any officer,
employee,
member or consultant of
the agency,
or by a resident of the jurisdiction.
(Section
87308.)
2.
Does the Fair Political Practices Commission maintain
copies of county conflict of interest codes?
Since the Fair Political Practices Commission is not the code
reviewing body for county conflict of interest codes,
county
governments are riot required to submit conflict of interest codes
to the Commission.
Should an agency send us a copy of the code
for informational purposes,
we do retain a copy of such code.
A
copy of the conflict of interest code for Butte County is enclosed
for your review.
You will note that the Butte County code does
designate positions within the Assessor’s Office.
3.
Does the conflict of interest code for the Alameda County
Assessor’s Office exceed the requirements of state law?
Government Code Section 87302 requires that a conflict of
interest code enumerate the positions within an agency,
other than
those specified in Section 87200,
which involve the making or
participation in the making
4
! of decisions which may have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic
interest and for each such enumerated position,
the specific types
of investments,
business positions,
interests in real property,
and sources of income which are reportable.
Section 87302 also
requires
a conflict of interest code to set out certain
miscellaneous provisions,
such as the monetary thresholds of
financial interests that are required to be reported; the types of
statements which are required; the reporting period covered by
those statements;
and the circumstances under which designated
employees must disqualify themselves from making or participating
in the making of decisions.
The conflict of interest code for the Alameda County
Assessor’s Office, at Section 4.01(D),
enumerates the positions
within the agency which appear to make or participate in the
making of decisions.
The code at the “Note” provides that the
designated positions listed are in a disclosure category requiring
the reporting of all sources of income, interests in real
property,
investments and business positions in business entities.
The Assessor’s Office incorporated Commission Regulation
18730 as the body of its code..
Regulation 18730 contains all of
the miscellaneous provisions described above,
such as the monetary
4’
Commission Regulation 18700
(enclosed) describes what it means
to make or participate in the making of decisions.


Page Four
Advice File No.
1-92—226
thresholds that financial interests are required to be reported,
the types of statements which are required, the reporting period
covered by those statements, the circumstances under which
designated employees must disqualify themselves from making or
participating in the making of decisions, etc.
When an agency incorporates Regulation 18730 as the body of
its code, all it need do to comply with Section 87302 is to create
the appendix of designated positions and disclosure categories.
The conflict of interest code for the Alameda County Assessor’s
Office appears to meet all of the requirements of Section 87302.
It should be noted,
though, that since the Commission has
no knowledge of the duties and responsibilities of the persons
designated,
we cannot determine if the assigned disclosure is too
broad.
However, if the duties of a designated employee are so
broad or indefinable that the requirements of Section 87309,
described above,
cannot be complied with,
the conflict of interest
code shall require the designated employee to comply with the
requirements of Article
2 of the Act.
Article 2,
commencing at
Section 87200,
requires the disclosure of all financial interests.
(Section 87310.)
4.
Is
a Report of Outside Employment part of the Political
Reform Act,
and if so,
how many other counties use such a report?
The conflict of interest code provisions appear in Section
4.01(C)
of the Office of Assessor Personnel Manual.
While the
Report of Outside Employment is in the same manual, it is not part
of the conflict of interest code.
The filing of the Report may be
in compliance with Revenue
& Taxation Code Section 1365,
which is
not part of the Act.
I hope this has answered your questions satisfactorily.
If I
may be of further assistance,
please feel free to call me at
(916)
322—5901.
JET/jt
cc.
John Scott,
MAI
Alameda County Assessor
inette E. Turvill
litical Reform Consultant
Legal Division

sections.conclusion:
1.
The Alameda County Board of Supervisors is the code
reviewing body for all agencies within Alameda County and has the
authority to approve, modify or reject the provisions of a
conflict of interest code within its jurisdiction.
2.
The Fair Political Practices Commission does not maintain
copies of conflict of interest codes for county governments.
3.
It appears that the Conflict of Interest Code for the
Alameda County Assessor’s Office does not exceed the requirements
of the Political Reform Act.
3
?
4.
The Political Reform Act does not require the filing of a
Report of Outside Employment.

The Alameda County Board of Supervisors is the code
reviewing body for all agencies within Alameda County and has the
authority to approve, modify or reject the provisions of a
conflict of interest code within its jurisdiction.
2.
The Fair Political Practices Commission does not maintain
copies of conflict of interest codes for county governments.
3.
It appears that the Conflict of Interest Code for the
Alameda County Assessor’s Office does not exceed the requirements
of the Political Reform Act.
3
?
4.
The Political Reform Act does not require the filing of a
Report of Outside Employment.
